sentences.text: First, we are going in the rain and we are freezing to this degree.
So the train had this big nails-- so if you lie next to the nail, your clothes was freezing to the nail, you know?
We arrived, we didn't know where, somewhere in the north.
We are unloaded-- it was already evening-- from the tracks.
And put-- walked-- brought by tracks, and brought into a camp, concentration camp, and all assembled [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in an area of the camp.
We were about thousands in the transport.
We were already mixed with the Russians.
This was October-- end of September or beginning of October, 1940.
It was more or less one-- one year, October, I think, one year in the concentration camp almost-- in the jails.
[SIGHS] Then this was a medical commission.
And I noticed colossal disorder, how they were getting people through.
So they determine that I am good for work.
The next days, they called us to work.
When they came to me, I came to-- I told them a lie, said the commission found me unable-- unsuitable for work.
It took them a month to find out that I was a liar.
During this period, I was left inside the camp.
The camp, the concentration camps in Russia, inside the camp, were run by the inmates-- but only by the inmates whom the Russian authorities trusted.
And they were the criminals.
They were political people, some of those people.
But I made friends with them during the month, when I was inside the camp-- because it was empty.
Because nobody was-- them, they are the administration end.
Nobody [INAUDIBLE] during the day.
And after  the month, when they came for me, the camps, inside the camp authority were making the decision on which brigade I go.
So they found me a comparatively lighter brigade to work.
We were on Kandalaksha on the White Sea in the polar region.
Kandalaksha was-- I transfer-- translate it into English.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Kandala were monocles.
This was a place where they were keeping prisoners in cells, as well.
It was on the Kola peninsula, the south of the Kola peninsula.
The Kola peninsula is extremely rich in minerals.
And they were building there-- we were--our camp was building a power station for aluminium refinery.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They were close-- it was supposed to be the biggest aluminium refinery in the world.
Anyway, after months working in the forest, I got pneumonia and was taken to the camp-- Lazaret was the camp, with high temperatures.
And while I was there, after a while, I was better.
I had a good pair of boots.
I gave the boots to somebody.
And for this, they let me in the hospital to work as a cleaner.
So at least I was warm.
From working as a cleaner, I progressed and became slowly an assistant to a doctor, and started seeing patients in my own right.
[LAUGHS] Then, I became the sanitary inspector for the camp.
So I had colossal powers.
So I was getting special food from the kitchen because I could make anybody to be thrown to out to work, you know-- which I never did.
But I had colossal authority.
And I made friends with the Russian inmates, as well.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Among the people in the camp was a Pole who was president of the National Legal Association of Poland, member of the Polish senate, president of Rosenheim, was one of the biggest cities of this, professor of philosophy of law, member of the Committee of Five.
He was running big Polish anti-Semitic party, National Democratic Party.
And this guy was old, he was around 60.
When we were coming from work, he was always coming to my bed and talking to me.
And I said to him on one day, I said to him why do you-- you have so many Poles here, why do you come to me.
You are-- you are leading the anti-Semitic party in Poland.
And he said, look, what do I have common with them?
I can't talk with them--I can talk with you.
[LAUGHS]
He was-- he study at a very high level.
We had very-- I don't want to repeat our discussions about situation of Jews, about his position and everything.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] On the 22nd of June, 1941, Germans attack Russia.
A few weeks earlier, at once came instruction that all the political prisoners who are on the special jobs have to go on ordinary jobs, because they can't be trusted.
So I was taken away from hospital and sent to work.
But, this time, I had already connections.
So also I was sent to work as a laborer outside where they were building houses for the future workers of this refinery.
I was put in charge-- illegal, not legally, but practically-- for receiving material which are delivered from the harbor, building materials.
This materials were delivered by Russians who were forcibly sent over there but who were not inmates of the camp.
And they were paid according to the quantity of materials they brought.
So I was signing for what they wanted and they were bringing me butter and all food items, you know?
Most probably, it would be lasting longer.
I signed for so much materials, there could be 10 such villages.
[LAUGHS] Then one day came orders that all the political inmates are being sent inside Russia, or to Siberia.
But, in Russia-- in Russia, you were nobody.
You didn't have your documents.
And they couldn't find my documents and documents of a number of other people, so we were not sent.
And we were taken together with the criminals and sent to the Russian front, into Finland, into this part of Finland was taken by the Russians in 1940 war from the Finnish.
And we found ourselves behind the army lines.
As we arrived for-- immediately, we were put to work.
There were very narrow roads and lakes in the Finnish part.
It was only one road going among the lakes and mountains.
And we were put to work to cut the roads so that Germans could not eventually come through with the tanks.
And when we arrived, we were working on it without sleep for 72 hours.
And, at this time, came a guy who was in charge of the medical-- Lazaret-- over the hospital, small hospital.
And he got me out to assist him.
Then two weeks later, this so-called hospital was a tent outside of the zone where the inmates were in the forest.
And there were two beds for eventual hospital cases.
But there were no hospital case.
First, he slept on one and I slept on the other, then he was moved somewhere else.
So I became the only medical guy for a camp of 7,000 people next to the front.
I was doing small surgery by night with a piece of timber soaked in kerosene and light.
And I was doing quite a bit of it.
Did I do it well or not?
I don't know.
But, definitely, most probably, I saved more people than I killed.
After over a week came a woman who was a Jewish woman, a doctor.
Her husband was in a very high position and in the 1938, there was a big-- started arresting, it was the people in high positions and so on.
He was-- her husband was arrested as well.
And they arrested the families.
And the families were with-- under the court, member of the family code without term.
And she was one of them.
And then, as a doctor, she was sent to the camp.
I met another-- I met a wife and the mother of Marshal Shaposhnikov.
It means the wife of the previous Marshal Shaposhnikov, and the wife of the Marshal Shaposhnikov who was with Gorbachev.
Not wife, the-- the mother of-- the wife of the Marshal Shaposhnikov who was commanding general of Moscow in starting this.
And he was the father of Shaposhnikov who was-- look, Molotov, who was foreign minister, and, later, for a while, prime minister of Russia, his wife was in concentration camp.
This what was going on in Russia, it was unbelievable.
Stalin killed his son-in-law who was Jewish.

[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Son of Stalin who was a member-- who was a prisoner of war in Germany, Hitler wanted to let him out, especially for propaganda reason, committed suicide, because he was frightened to-- he knew that he will be killed by his father when he comes out.
Anyway, this was the camp story.
One day, when I was sitting in front of-- during the day, I had nothing to do because in the morning I was standing and looking-- somebody was sick, I was supposed to let him out for work site, too.
I was sitting in-- this was polar summer.
I was seeing in from of the tent and I saw a high officer coming with a little suitcase.
And the guy stopped, looked at me, and started asking me questions-- asking who I am, where I came from, what my parents were doing, and so on.
And then he went to see the commanding officer of the camp.
The head of the medical section was the wife of the commanding officer and was very kind to me.
And after about half an hour, she came running and said, look, we have problem.
He is the new political officer with colossal powers.
He had bigger powers than my husband.
And he wants you immediately removed from this job, because he says that you have medicines, over there, you can poison the whole camp.
And my husband tried, but he can't do anything.
And you will have to be moved.
So nothing was to-- it couldn't be helped.
I was waiting the next day, nothing happened.
In the afternoon, this guy came and started talking to me again.
And he was already-- we started-- we started being friendly.
And I was never moved.
He was coming everyday to talk to me.
We were talking very-- not political, but very openly about things.

And when, later, came the amnesty for us, when the war-- when Sikorsky was in Moscow and signed the pact with Stalin, as a matter of fact, I found out about the amnesty-- one day, I was in the tent and came a Russian general, a Jew, was an old Chekist from Dzerzhinsky's days.
I first time saw him and he said to me, do you want to go to fight with Mr.
Sikorsky.
I couldn't understand-- he said there's a amnesty for you.
You are going to join the Polish army under Russian command and you will fight the Germans.
And, really, we were called in two days later, and put into trucks and sent back to the harbor place, the harbor on the White Sea.
When we were brought to the trucks, I was put in charge.
There was about 700 of us.
I was put in charge of them.
And the political officer came to me and said to me, look, you will soon maybe leave Russia and you will have bad memories about Russia because you were not treated very well.
I said, oh.
And he said, look, when we will have this bad memories and be critical of everything, try to be non-- not partial, and think about the good sides, as well.
Think about the education we gave to people, think about the medical system, which didn't exist, we gave to people.
Not everything was wrong here.
And do you know, any time I talk about Russia now, this words come to me back?
Anyway, I was in charge of this group of 700.
And I didn't know where the Polish army is forming.
And we started going south to look for the Polish army.
And we're going everywhere.
We are contacting the Russian security services because they were-- they couldn't tell us.
They didn't know themselves much.
And finally, I found the Polish army in the Urals.
And when we reported, when I brought the-- the Poles were escaping on the way.
They said, we had enough.
We were in jail.
We are not going to fight.
We should have some good time, as well.
When I brought the people in towards the camp, were put in front of the commission.
And everyone-- all Jews were thrown out.
I was given category [NON-ENGLISH], which was somebody without two arms and two legs.
And then, a few days later, I was put in the transport without papers and sent south.
And I came to Uzbekistan.
It was a long way down, which is north of Afghanistan, to Kogon.
And then some-- I didn't have any money because once they release me from the camp, they give you 25 rubles, which was nothing.
And this 25 rubles was stolen from my pocket on the-- in-- on the way-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in Alma-ata.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] So I found myself in Kogon without a penny.
In the train, there were people coming from Buzurg, where was the headquarters of the Polish army.
And they were some friends over there whom I knew, but they have something because they were not in concentration camps.
They were sent to different places to live, but nobody helped me.
I didn't have a penny.
They went to-- they decided to go to Bukhara to live.
And they told me that my cousin is in Bukhara now.
So I didn't have money for train or anything, so I carried the luggage and they gave me 5 rubles.
For this, I first went and cut my hair.
A secondly, I bought a soup, and had enough for the ticket.
I went to Bukhara, and this was my worst period because I started dying from hunger.
My cousin didn't help me, my first cousin.
And also, he had money for me from an uncle, which I found out only after the war.
I didn't have any bread ration, because the Poles threw me out of the army without giving me any documents, and I couldn't get any rations without documents.
So I was-- for half a year, I didn't eat bread.
I was standing in the queues for bread for others.
And, for this, I was paid with a bit of bread, which was I was selling on the black market because it wouldn't last me for long and was buying carrots, which was the cheapest thing.
So for half a year, I was eating carrots only.
My weight came to below 80 pounds.
I couldn't walk.
In the morning, I couldn't open my eyes.
And then the Polish army came over to the south, the whole Polish army.
And I volunteered again and I was sent to the Polish army.
I arrived in [NON-ENGLISH], which was one of the places that the army was.
I was thrown out of there and not accepted again.
But I was accepted provisionally.
I was working in the mobilization station.
And there was a story that they took people with high education and put them in separate groups-- they will be moving with others.
And it didn't help, because they didn't take us.
There was two groups which were leaving Russia.
One group left in May, I think, or April.
And the other one in August.
I was not in the one, I was not in the other.
But I was working in the mobilization station, so I had access to documents.
I stole a document with the-- documents, travel documents.
And I fill it for myself and 19 others.
And we got, illegally, to-- on the train and we were travelling towards Krasnoyarsk, which was a harbor on the Caspian Sea, from which transport to Iran were going, of the army and families.
As a matter of fact, there were family registration in [NON-ENGLISH].
And I had a friend who was later the leader in Israel, one of the leaders of Likud-- Bader, from Krakow.
And he told me to declare myself non-Jewish.
He did the same.
And he got through.
His-- his cousin was a colonel in the Polish army, baptized.
And Bader got through, he went to Israel and had became one of the top politicians in Israel, the oldest member of the parliament.
When we came to-- when we got to on the train, we came to Ashgabat.
And then we heard there's a Polish mission there.
So I went to the Polish mission and talked to the colonel in charge.
And he said to me, wonderful, you go-- where is your train?
You go back to your car, where you are, where is the railway car, and we will send you food and we will take care of you.
Do you know what he did?
He informed the Russian militzia and they came and arrested us.
So we were thrown into jail again on the station of Krasnoyarsk railway station.
And then, by night, they came and took us out of jail and put us on the train to Krasnoyarsk.
With Russians, you never knew what happened.
So I got to Krasnoyarsk.
And, summer, I got on of one of the boats were going to Iran.
And again, the Polish gendarmerie took me from this and give me in the hands of the Russians.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] From there, I came back to Bukhara.
And I didn't want to-- shortly after I arrive in Bukhara, I had already papers because I had my release papers from the army.
So I could get a bread ration.

Shortly after arrival, started the-- they wanted to force those who didn't leave with the army to take Russian citizenship on the basis that where they had the premises on the Ukraine and Belarus, 90-odd percent voted, Russians voting, for alliance with Russia.
So I must have been one of them.
Obviously, this why I was-- I had to take Russian citizenship.
So they put me in jail because I didn't want to take it.
I was, for a few weeks, in jail.
And then I saw there is-- that I'm going to the concentration camp again, so I signed for the citizenship.
After I sign for the citizenship, I got the work as a laborer.
And in a very short period, I was head of the Russian enterprise.
And I started living very well.
You see, in Russia, everybody was stealing, from bottom to top.
I was stealing, too, but I have something to steal-- kerosene and soap, which is gold in the war times.
So I was doing very well, with the help of the police, and Communist Party, and everybody.
We shared all of it among us, like the police inquiry now.
[LAUGHS] And then the Polish army started-- was formed under Russian command.
And when I saw that they are coming already close to Polish border, I volunteered.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I met my wife in Bukhara.
She was from Romania.
And I married her on the 15th of July.
And on the 22nd of July, I left her.
I remember, I went to the commission, medical commission.
There was a woman who was a doctor.
She was the sister of Henry Yagoda.
You know who Yagoda was?
After Genrikh, he was the head of the Cheka.
He was later bumped off by Stalin, but he killed many people.
His sister was-- he was a Jew.
His sister was a doctor.
When she saw me, she knew me.
She said, Towarisch Finkelstein, do you want that I declare you unsuitable for the army?
I said, God forbid, don't touch it.
[LAUGHS] I want to go.
So I joined the army.
I came to-- At that stage, did you have any idea of what had-- what was happening in Poland, what had happened to your family?
No.
We didn' have any clue, no.
We didn't even know rumors, nothing.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I came to-- to Zhytomyr on the Ukraine, or next to Zhytomyr.
And I joined the Polish army there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was not-- they wanted me first in the-- as a lawyer in the office of the solicitor general of the army.
And I didn't want to go because I knew this was a political job.
So I was still not properly mobilized.
And one day, they were organizing a new unit and I got into this unit.
And the commanding officer was Russian and he couldn't speak Polish.
So I immediately became his-- next to him.
And, after a while, I was in charge of the unit.
And I was-- we were depending immediately-- we were under the general commander of the Polish army.
We were attached to general commander's Polish army.
And I was doing very well there, so that when the war was over, three of the departments of the Polish army wanted me.
And I knew that this will by my end.
So after the war, I went to the medical commission, bribed them, and was declared unfit for the army.
I had already the appointment-- and it was an appointment with the rank of a full colonel, with this that the promise that in six months I would be a general.
When I came to the personnel office, I was Polish army to the head of personal office with this release.
He looked at it and said to me, you bastard.
And I said, right, it was nice knowing you.
[LAUGHS] I had the release in both name, Finklisht and Darin, because I didn't know-- I didn't want to ever to be-- to that they have against me that I was a deserter, you know?
And this was end of my Polish army story.
Now, I find-- during the army days, I tried to bring out my wife.
So, first of all, I went to the commanding general of the medical corps who was Russian.
And he told me there is no-- and I wanted him to draft my wife as a medical nurse.
And he told me that this was stopped, so he couldn't do it.

The next thing, towards the end of the war, I got a Russian major who was second in charge of the financial department of the Polish army, and I got for him a leave and sent him to Russia, to Bukhara, to get my wife.

And I arranged with a Russian colonel, who was in charge of a transportation unit, that if they bring him to-- her to the border between Russia and Poland, he will put a Russian uniform on her and bring her in a truck over.
But the guy did stupid things, this major, and he was for a few months away.
After a few months, he came back without my wife.
He was asking the security service for a visit to Poland.
Security service told me we can't give any visas to Poland.
This is the foreign office can do it.
So he was drinking all the time over there and then he came back without her.
We are still going through the war time, yes?
